bibliographic.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne), in The Works of Thomas Hearne, M.A., vols. III-IV (London: printed for Samuel Bagster, 1810).
bibliographic.container_title: The Works of Thomas Hearne, M.A.
bibliographic.pages: ccxxvi, 230, [2], 235-714, 4 pages, continuously paged across two volumes
bibliographic.title: Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne)

entities.description: The immediate print source: volumes III-IV of The Works of Thomas Hearne. It resets Hearne's 1725 edition in new type while retaining the old title page wording and the continuously paged Middle English text, notes, glossary, index, and appended tracts. Its printing colophons and pagination distinguish it from the 1725 first edition.
entities.label: Bagster reset reprint of Hearne's Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (1810)
notes: Mercier and Chervet printing colophons appear on pages 230 and 714.

The cataloged Mannyng selection is printed at pages 1-341. The Glastonbury roll begins at page 343; later appendices concern Scrooby, Roman antiquities, and Stonehenge, followed by glossary and index.
The reset uses modern long-s-free typography and is not a photographic facsimile of 1725.

rights.basis: The reset reprint was first published in 1810 and is far older than the maximum U.S. term for works first published before 1978; Internet Archive's Wellcome-derived item also bears the Public Domain Mark.
rights.component: The 1810 reset text, typography, arrangement, and the underlying 1725 and medieval content

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Hearne, ed., Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne), from the Death of Cadwalader to the End of K. Edward the First's Reign, 2 vols. (Oxford: printed at the Theater, 1725).
bibliographic.pages: ccxxxii, 230, [2], 235-722, [2] pages, continuously paged across two volumes
bibliographic.title: Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne)

entities.description: Hearne's two-volume first publication of Robert Mannyng's Part II, embedded in a composite antiquarian publication. Hearne transcribed the Middle English text from the Inner Temple manuscript, deliberately omitted Part I, and added Anglo-Norman comparison notes after consulting three Langtoft manuscripts.
entities.label: Thomas Hearne's first edition of Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (1725)
notes: The title page says the Middle English text was transcribed and first published from a manuscript in the Inner Temple Library.

The composite also contains an extensive publisher's preface and appendix, a Glastonbury survey, John Slacke's Scrooby hospital account, two anonymous antiquarian tracts, glossary, index, subscribers, and an advertisement.
Hearne's phrase 'Peter Langtoft's Chronicle' names the source and publication, but the continuous English verse at pages 1-341 is Mannyng's Part II.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1725 and is far older than the maximum U.S. term for works first published before 1978; the exact edition is publicly reproduced by Internet Archive.
rights.component: Hearne's 1725 transcription, edition, apparatus, and the underlying medieval texts

access.entityLabel: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7
notes: This is an exact scholarly bibliography entry maintained by U-M, not an Inner Temple-owned item catalog record. Inner Temple's collection-level page is used only for physical consultation, microfilm supply, photography, and publication policy.

access.accessMethod: Apply in writing to the Librarian with research purpose and, where appropriate, an academic recommendation; request Petyt MS 511, vol. 7 for consultation
access.entityLabel: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7

access.accessMethod: Request information from Inner Temple Library about the 1978 World Microfilms edition and purchase of microcopies
access.entityLabel: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7
access.format: Microfilm copy; exact reel and current supply route require confirmation

dateStatements.label: recent linguistic assessment
dateStatements.notes: Attributed to Sullens's codicological discussion and eLALME profile 38 by Pons-Sanz and Roberts.
dateStatements.value: second half of the fourteenth century, possibly about 1375

entities.description: The parchment manuscript from which Hearne transcribed Mannyng's complete Part II. Hearne received it on loan from the Inner Temple through John Anstis and John Bridges. The Middle English Compendium records one hand, LALME Linguistic Profile 38, and Lincolnshire localization; recent linguistic scholarship dates it to the second half of the fourteenth century, possibly about 1375, while the MED bibliography uses about 1400.

holding.notes: The official Inner Temple collection page confirms the extant Petyt collection and manuscript access policy but is not an exact item catalog record.

entities.label: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7

physicalDescription.extent: A manuscript of Mannyng's complete two-part Chronicle; exact foliation was not established from a current public item record
notes: Hearne describes the copy as complete from beginning to end and uses it as the sole named Middle English base for the published Part II.
The MED bibliography records one hand and a Lincolnshire scribal language; this witness localization is not transferred to every textual layer of the abstract work.

rights.basis: The manuscript and text were created in the fourteenth century and Hearne's transcription was published in 1725; this statement does not cover modern photography, repository permissions, or contractual access conditions.
rights.component: The medieval text embodied in Petyt MS 511, vol. 7

rights.basis: Inner Temple permits non-flash research photography for private study but requires prior written permission for publication; no complete image set with item-specific reuse license was found.
rights.component: Any modern photographs, scans, or microfilm frames of Petyt MS 511, vol. 7
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse

access.accessMethod: Contact the College of Arms about consultation, catalog detail, or reproduction for Arundel MS LXI; no exact public item record or complete surrogate was found
access.entityLabel: College of Arms, Arundel MS LXI

entities.description: An Anglo-Norman manuscript of Pierre de Langtoft's Chronicle used by Hearne for specimens and comparison notes. Hearne says it had belonged to Ralph Sheldon and judged it the oldest of the three French manuscripts he consulted. Wright identifies the current shelfmark as Arundel MS LXI, lettered Vincent, and describes Langtoft's Chronicle as occupying all but the first two leaves.

holding.notes: The official College of Arms page confirms that its Arundel manuscripts are medieval chronicles and histories but does not expose an exact online record for LXI.

entities.label: College of Arms, Arundel MS LXI

physicalDescription.extent: Langtoft's Chronicle occupies the volume except for the first two leaves, which contain a king list and other entries
notes: Wright describes the copy as generally close to his manuscript A but with many readings shared with C; he does not treat it as the universal base of Langtoft's text.

rights.basis: The official collection page confirms the holding but exposes neither an exact image record nor item-specific reproduction and reuse terms.
rights.component: Any modern photographs or scans of College of Arms Arundel MS LXI
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse

access.accessMethod: No current shelfmark or exact public catalog match was identified in targeted College of Arms, British Library, Bodleian/MarCO, and All Souls searches
access.entityLabel: John Anstis's complete manuscript of Langtoft's Chronicle

entities.description: One of two Anglo-Norman Langtoft manuscripts owned by John Anstis and bound together in a single volume when lent to Hearne. This copy continued through the death of Edward I and supplied comparison readings in Hearne's notes. No current shelfmark was identified in the reviewed public sources.
entities.label: John Anstis's complete manuscript of Langtoft's Chronicle

physicalDescription.extent: Complete through the death of Edward I; bound with Anstis's partial copy in Hearne's time
physicalDescription.format: Manuscript codex or codicological unit; material and present binding not established

access.accessMethod: No current shelfmark or exact public catalog match was identified in targeted College of Arms, British Library, Bodleian/MarCO, and All Souls searches
access.entityLabel: John Anstis's partial Part II manuscript of Langtoft's Chronicle

entities.description: The second Anglo-Norman Langtoft manuscript owned by John Anstis and bound with his complete copy. Hearne says it contained only Part II and ended at material corresponding to his printed page 287. It supplied comparison readings in the notes. No current shelfmark was identified in the reviewed public sources.

identifiers.value: Anstis partial Langtoft Part II copy

entities.label: John Anstis's partial Part II manuscript of Langtoft's Chronicle

physicalDescription.extent: Part II only, ending before the death of Edward I at material corresponding to Hearne page 287
physicalDescription.format: Manuscript codex or codicological unit; material and present binding not established

entities.description: Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman verse history of Britain and England through Edward I. Mannyng's Part II chiefly translates and adapts the corresponding later portion, with additions absent from Langtoft.
entities.label: The Chronicle of Pierre de Langtoft
notes: This is a source-work description, not an assertion that Mannyng used any one of Hearne's three surviving French collation manuscripts as his physical exemplar.

next_steps: Request a written interpretation from U-M Library before redistributing the XML or a close derivative as a content provider.
Keep copyright status, page-image terms, electronic-markup terms, and repository licensing separate in downstream reuse decisions.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede, clarify, or coexist with the copyright and distribution caution embedded in the 2006 XML?

next_steps: Inspect an annotated copy of the 12-13 December 1768 Anstis sale catalogue and trace the relevant lot purchasers.
Compare Hearne's quoted readings, the complete-copy ending, and the partial-copy ending near page 287 against current Langtoft manuscript censuses.
Enquire with the College of Arms, British Library, Bodleian Library, and All Souls rather than describing either copy as lost.
openQuestions.question: What are the current shelfmarks, holdings, and survival status of the two Anglo-Norman Langtoft manuscripts that John Anstis owned bound together and lent to Hearne?

next_steps: Ask Inner Temple Library for its current item-level description, condition and foliation data, the exact 1978 microfilm reel, and any existing digital photography.
Obtain item-specific written publication permission and image terms before reusing manuscript photographs.
Check the Bentley Historical Library MED Records Box 70 photostat as a research surrogate without assuming publication rights.
openQuestions.question: Is there an exact current Inner Temple item record or complete institutional digital surrogate for Petyt MS 511, vol. 7, and what written terms would govern publication of its images?

next_steps: Compare Sullens's codicological discussion, Davies's 1972 catalog entry, and current eLALME profile 38 directly.
Record palaeographical and linguistic dating as attributed claims and do not collapse them into a false consensus.
openQuestions.question: Should Petyt MS 511, vol. 7 be dated about 1375, broadly to the second half of the fourteenth century, or around 1400?

next_steps: Request the exact catalog entry and reproduction options from the College of Arms.
Do not generalize access or image terms from the collection-level webpage.
openQuestions.question: Does the College of Arms have an exact public or mediated catalog record, complete surrogate, and item-specific reuse terms for Arundel MS LXI?

reviewNotes: The primary Middle English text is Robert Mannyng's Part II. The inherited Peter Langtoft AUTHOR label describes its source tradition and Hearne's publication title, not the English work's intellectual authorship.
Hearne explicitly selected Part II and omitted Part I; the surrounding Glastonbury, Scrooby, Roman-antiquities, and Stonehenge texts are separate components of the composite edition.
The College of Arms and Anstis French manuscripts supplied Anglo-Norman comparison notes only. They are separate collation witnesses, not parallel intrinsic parts or Middle English base witnesses.

The Lambeth Palace copy known to Hearne belongs to the wider Mannyng tradition, but the reviewed preface does not show that it supplied the printed Part II text; it is therefore not placed upstream of the edition.
The 1810 publication is a reset reprint, not a facsimile of 1725. Its changed preliminaries, final pagination, and printing colophons remain edition-specific.
No exact current Inner Temple online item record was found for Petyt 511, vol. 7. The official collection page supports holding and access policy but is not used as holding.catalog_url.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item ABA2096.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected-OCR encoding linked to 946 page images of the 1810 Bagster reprint in The Works of Thomas Hearne, volumes III-IV. That reset reprint preserves Thomas Hearne's 1725 first edition of Robert Mannyng's complete Part II, printed on pages 1-341 within a two-volume composite. Hearne transcribed the Middle English text from Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7. After printing began, he used three Anglo-Norman Langtoft manuscripts only for comparative specimens and notes: the extant College of Arms Arundel MS LXI and two manuscripts then owned and bound together by John Anstis whose current shelfmarks remain unidentified. The searchable U-M text retains Part II and Hearne's embedded apparatus but omits most surrounding preface, glossary, index, and appended antiquarian tracts, while retaining their page-image milestones.

catalogSummary.title: The Chronicle, Part II

catalogingSubject.label: The Chronicle, Part II
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete second part of Robert Mannyng's Middle English verse Chronicle (also called the Story of Inglande), from Cadwaladr through the death of Edward I, as selected and first printed by Thomas Hearne. This record does not describe Mannyng's Part I, Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle as a separate work, the whole 1725 or 1810 composite publication, Hearne's appended antiquarian tracts, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Part II: Cadwaladr to the death of Edward I
notes: The XML has no reliable intrinsic chapter hierarchy. Its 304 LG containers are representation units, not asserted authorial chapters.
contentParts.summary: Mannyng's Middle English continuation of English history, chiefly translating and adapting the second part of Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle.

dateStatements.display: During the reign of Edward III, completed on the Ides of May (15 May) 1338
notes: The Petyt colophon has inconsistent dominical-letter and regnal-year details, but scholarship identifies the intended year as 1338, when the Ides of May fell on Friday. The year range records Edward III's reign, not a claim that all of Part II was continuously composed from 1327.

dateStatements.display: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7, second half of the fourteenth century to around 1400; possibly about 1375
notes: This is a witness date, not the composition date. The MED bibliography uses about 1400, while recent linguistic scholarship cites a second-half-fourteenth-century date, possibly about 1375.

extent.description: One continuous verse work. The XML contains 8,365 L elements overall: 8,357 direct lines in the main poem plus eight L elements nested inside Hearne's footnotes. Modern reference descriptions commonly report 8,358 lines for Part II, so the value here is explicitly an encoding-specific metric rather than a universal critical line count.

formStatements.description: A continuous Middle English rhymed verse chronicle; Hearne's prose and multilingual footnotes are edition-level apparatus.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English commentary, Latin formulas, and Anglo-Norman comparison passages belong to Hearne's edition and apparatus and do not change the cataloged work's primary language.

The XML header inherits a generic catalog title and AUTHOR entry for Peter of Langtoft. Direct inspection shows that the encoded Middle English body is Robert Mannyng's Part II; Langtoft is the source author, not the author of this English wording.
The XML's subject keywords describe the whole composite 1725/1810 publication. Glastonbury Abbey, Scrooby, Roman antiquities, and Stonehenge are not subjects of the cataloged Part II.
No date, dialect, holding, access condition, or image-rights statement belonging to Petyt 511 or the three French collation witnesses is assigned wholesale to the abstract work.

next_steps: Consult J. C. Davies's 1972 Inner Temple manuscript catalogue entry, Sullens's codicological discussion, and current eLALME profile 38 side by side.
Keep the witness date separate from Mannyng's 1338 composition date and do not prefer the narrowest date without direct palaeographical evidence.
openQuestions.question: How should the published datings of Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7—possibly about 1375 and around 1400—be reconciled?

next_steps: Compare the final lineation in Petyt 511, Hearne 1725/1810, and Sullens 1996.
Retain the explicit XML metric until a specific omitted, joined, or differently counted line is identified.
openQuestions.question: Why does the XML contain 8,357 direct poem lines when modern descriptions commonly give 8,358 lines for Part II?

notes: Mannyng's autobiographical prologue associates his writing of the Story with the Gilbertine house at Sixhills, and scholarship says he finished the chronicle there. This does not prove that every stage of composition occurred at Sixhills. London and Oxford are edition or repository places, not medieval composition places.
Authorship here names the creator of the Middle English work, not the author of its principal Anglo-Norman source.

scope.description: Chiefly translates the second part of Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle

notes: Mannyng adds material from other written and oral sources and expands or reshapes Langtoft; 'translation' alone would understate the work.

scope.description: Hearne's 1725 edited selection of Part II, retained in the 1810 reset reprint
Transcription of Mannyng's Part II from Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7

notes: The French manuscripts supplied comparison material in the footnotes; they were not base witnesses for Mannyng's Middle English text.

scope.description: Anglo-Norman specimens and comparison notes from three French Langtoft manuscripts
Preface and embedded historical, textual, and Anglo-Norman notes surrounding the edited poem

subjects.label: Reign of Edward I and Anglo-Scottish conflict

scope.description: The chronicle's extended concluding historical sequence

summaries.value: Part II of Robert Mannyng's Chronicle recounts English history from the transition after Cadwaladr through the death and burial of Edward I. It follows Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle closely while adding, expanding, and reshaping material from other written and oral traditions. Mannyng presents the past through sequences of rulers, wars, political counsel, moral judgment, and contested succession, ending with his authorial leave-taking and a dated colophon.

Hearne deliberately omitted Mannyng's Part I and first published only Part II. The U-M encoding narrows the composite book further: its searchable text retains Part II on printed pages 1-341 and Hearne's embedded notes, while most of the publisher's preface, glossary, index, Glastonbury roll, Scrooby account, Roman-antiquities tract, and Stonehenge tract survive only as omitted-matter page milestones or linked page images.

notes: The modern preferred title identifies the edited selection. Folger catalogs Sullens's complete-work edition as The Chronicle; 'Part II' prevents this record from being mistaken for the whole two-part work.

titles.value: The Story of Inglande, Part II

notes: Hearne's publication title foregrounds Langtoft, but the printed Middle English text is Mannyng's translation and adaptation.
titles.value: Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne), from the Death of Cadwalader to the End of King Edward the First's Reign

work.author: Robert Mannyng of Brunne
work.dateDisplay: Composed during the reign of Edward III and completed 15 May 1338

publication.embeddedTitle: Peter Langtoft's Chronicle, (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne) from the death of Cadwalader to the end of K. Edward the First's reign. Transcrib'd, and now first publish'd, from a ms. in the Inner-Temple Library by Thomas Hearne, M.A. To which are added, besides a glossary and other curious papers, (1) A roll concerning Glastonbury abbey, being a survey of all the estates belonging to that house at the dissolution, taken by King Hen. the Eigth's order and for his use. (2) An account of the hospital of St. Mary Magdalen near Scroby in Nottinghamshire, by John Slacke, master of that hospital. (3) Two tracts by an anonymous author; the first relating to Roman antiquities, near Conquest in Somersetshire, the second concerning Stonehenge. Oxford, Printed at the Theater, 1725.

work.regionDisplay: Completed at Sixhills, Lincolnshire, England; full composition geography not established

subjects.label: Reign of Edward I and Anglo-Scottish conflict

work.summary: Part II of Robert Mannyng's Chronicle recounts English history from the transition after Cadwaladr through the death and burial of Edward I. It follows Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle closely while adding, expanding, and reshaping material from other written and oral traditions. Mannyng presents the past through sequences of rulers, wars, political counsel, moral judgment, and contested succession, ending with his authorial leave-taking and a dated colophon.
